questions.question:
Which one of the first order predicate calculus statements given below correctly expresses the following English statement?

Tigers and lions attack if they are hungry or threatened.

A. forall y [Tiger(x) ^ Lion(x)] -> [(Hungry(y) v Threatened(y)) -> Attack(x)]
B. forall x [Tiger(x) ^ Lion(x)] -> [(Hungry(x) v Threatened(x)) -> Attack(x)]
C. forall x [(Tiger(x) v Lion(x)) -> ((Hungry(x) ^ Threatened(x)) -> Attack(x))]
D. forall x [(Tiger(x) v Lion(x)) -> ((Hungry(x) v Threatened(x)) -> Attack(x))]
questions.answer: D. forall x [(Tiger(x) v Lion(x)) -> ((Hungry(x) v Threatened(x)) -> Attack(x))]
questions.to_find: correct FOL translation of Tigers and lions attack if they are hungry or threatened

understand.plain: The phrase 'tigers and lions' means anything that is a tiger OR a lion — it is a collective subject covering both classes. Option D uses disjunction for both the subject class (Tiger(x) v Lion(x)) and the attack condition (Hungry(x) v Threatened(x)), which exactly captures the English statement. Options A and B use conjunction for the subject class, and option C requires both hungry AND threatened simultaneously.

keywords.term: Tigers and lions as collective subject
keywords.explain: 'Tigers and lions' describes the union of two animal classes: anything that is a tiger OR a lion. No single creature needs to be both. So Tiger(x) v Lion(x) is correct; Tiger(x) ^ Lion(x) is wrong.
keywords.example: Tiger(x) v Lion(x) is correct; Tiger(x) ^ Lion(x) is wrong since nothing is simultaneously both

keywords.explain: 'X attacks if Y' means Y is the antecedent: Y -> Attack(X). The 'if' clause is the condition that must hold for the attack to happen.
keywords.example: 'Attacks if hungry or threatened' = (Hungry(x) v Threatened(x)) -> Attack(x)

keywords.explain: Either being hungry alone or threatened alone is sufficient to trigger attack, so the condition uses disjunction (v). Option C uses conjunction (^) requiring both simultaneously — too restrictive.
keywords.example: Hungry(x) v Threatened(x) — either condition alone is enough

understand.visual_alt: Two overlapping circles for Tiger and Lion forming their union, with an arrow to Attack when Hungry or Threatened holds for any member.

given.aim: Translate 'Tigers and lions attack if they are hungry or threatened' into first order predicate logic.

terms.example: Tiger(tony) means tony is a tiger
terms.connects: part of the subject class: animals that are tigers or lions

terms.example: Lion(simba) means simba is a lion
terms.connects: the other part of the subject class alongside Tiger(x)

terms.meaning: x is hungry or x is threatened

terms.connects: the conclusion when the hunger or threat condition is met

given.plan: Use forall x. The outer implication: if x is a tiger or a lion, then if x is hungry or threatened, x attacks. Use v (disjunction) for both the subject class and the attack condition.

steps.title: Identify subject class using disjunction
steps.apply: 'Tigers and lions' is the union class: Tiger(x) v Lion(x). Options A and B use Tiger(x) ^ Lion(x) — a classic trap since no creature is simultaneously both. The conjunction makes the antecedent always false, yielding a vacuously true but meaningless formula.
steps.note: English 'and' joining two class names as a collective subject maps to OR (v) in FOL.

steps.title: Encode the attack condition with disjunction
steps.apply: 'Attack if hungry or threatened' = (Hungry(x) v Threatened(x)) -> Attack(x). Full formula: forall x [(Tiger(x) v Lion(x)) -> ((Hungry(x) v Threatened(x)) -> Attack(x))]. This is option D. Option C uses ^ for the condition — too restrictive.
steps.note: Option C's Hungry(x) ^ Threatened(x) requires both simultaneously — a stricter incorrect interpretation.

solution.result: forall x [(Tiger(x) v Lion(x)) -> ((Hungry(x) v Threatened(x)) -> Attack(x))] — option D.

questions.formula_note: The fol-and-classes-or rule is key: collective 'A and B' as subject in English maps to disjunction A(x) v B(x) in FOL. Combined with the when-implication rule for the attack condition, option D is uniquely correct.

questions.question:
Let Graph(x) be a predicate which denotes that x is a graph. Let Connected(x) be a predicate which denotes that x is connected. Which of the following first order logic sentences DOES NOT represent the statement: 'Not every graph is connected'?

A. ~forall x [Graph(x) -> Connected(x)]
B. exists x [Graph(x) ^ ~Connected(x)]
C. ~forall x [Graph(x) ^ Connected(x)]
D. forall x [Graph(x) -> ~Connected(x)]
questions.to_find: which option does NOT represent 'not every graph is connected'

understand.plain: 'Not every graph is connected' means there exists at least one graph that is not connected. Options A and B are logically equivalent direct translations. Option C is weaker but still consistent. Option D says 'every graph is not connected' meaning all graphs are disconnected — a completely different and much stronger claim.

keywords.explain: 'Not every A is B' means at least one A fails to be B. 'No A is B' means all A fail. These are very different claims.
keywords.example: 'Not every student passes' (some fail) vs 'No student passes' (all fail)

keywords.explain: ~forall x P(x) = exists x ~P(x). Pushing the negation past the universal quantifier flips it to existential.
keywords.example: ~forall x [G(x) -> C(x)] = exists x [G(x) ^ ~C(x)] — options A and B are equivalent

keywords.explain: forall x [G(x) -> ~C(x)] universally denies connectivity to all graphs. This is 'no graph is connected', far stronger than 'not every graph is connected'.
keywords.example: Option D: every graph is disconnected — completely different from 'some graph is disconnected'

understand.visual_alt: Collection of graph icons: most inside a 'Connected' region and one outside it, illustrating that not every graph is connected (but some are).

given.aim: Identify which FOL formula does NOT correctly represent 'not every graph is connected'.

terms.connects: domain restrictor in the outer implication

terms.example: Connected(G1) — G1 has a path between every pair of vertices
terms.connects: the property being selectively negated in the target statement
proves A and B are equivalent representations

given.plan: Target: 'not every graph is connected' = exists x [Graph(x) ^ ~Connected(x)]. Apply quantifier negation to verify A and B, then show D says all graphs are disconnected.

steps.title: Verify options A, B, C represent the target
steps.apply: A: ~forall x [G(x) -> C(x)] = exists x [G(x) ^ ~C(x)] — some graph is disconnected. B: directly states exists x [G(x) ^ ~C(x)]. A and B are equivalent. C: ~forall x [G(x) ^ C(x)] = exists x [~G(x) v ~C(x)] — weaker but still consistent. All three represent the target.
steps.note: A and B are precisely equivalent via quantifier negation plus implication-as-disjunction.

steps.title: Expose option D as the wrong formula
steps.apply: D: forall x [G(x) -> ~C(x)] means for every x, if x is a graph then x is NOT connected — i.e., every single graph is disconnected. This is 'no graph is connected', far stronger than the target 'not every graph is connected'.
steps.note: D says all graphs are disconnected; the target only says at least one graph is disconnected.

solution.result: Option D — forall x [Graph(x) -> ~Connected(x)] — does NOT represent 'not every graph is connected'. It claims all graphs are disconnected.

questions.formula_note: Quantifier negation (fol-quantifier-negation) shows A and B are equivalent; D is exposed as wrong because forall x [G(x) -> ~C(x)] universally denies connectivity to all graphs.

questions.question:
Let fsa(x) and pda(y) be two predicates such that fsa(x) means x is a finite state automaton and pda(y) means y is a pushdown automaton. Let equiv(x, y) be another predicate such that equiv(x, y) means x and y are equivalent. Which of the following first order logic statements represent the following: 'Each finite state automaton has an equivalent pushdown automaton'?

A. (forall x fsa(x)) -> (exists y pda(y) ^ equiv(x, y))
B. ~exists x fsa(x) -> ~(exists y pda(y) ^ equiv(x, y))
C. forall x [fsa(x) -> (exists y (pda(y) ^ equiv(x, y)))]
D. forall x exists y [fsa(x) ^ pda(y) ^ equiv(x, y)]
questions.answer: C. forall x [fsa(x) -> (exists y (pda(y) ^ equiv(x, y)))]
questions.to_find: correct FOL formula for 'each FSA has an equivalent PDA'

understand.plain: The statement says: for every FSA x, there exists a PDA y that is equivalent to x. This fits the standard 'every A has a B' FOL pattern: forall x [fsa(x) -> exists y (pda(y) ^ equiv(x,y))]. Option C matches exactly. The official GATE key marks this question as excluded (X), but C is the genuinely correct answer. Option D uses conjunction instead of implication, demanding every domain object be an FSA.

keywords.term: 'Every A has a B' FOL pattern
keywords.explain: The standard translation is forall x [A(x) -> exists y (B(y) ^ Relation(x,y))]. Universal over A uses implication; existential over B uses conjunction.
The PDA y that witnesses equivalence depends on which FSA x is chosen, so 'exists y' must appear inside 'forall x' — each FSA gets its own witness PDA.
keywords.example: forall x [fsa(x) -> EXISTS y (pda(y) ^ equiv(x,y))] — y is found after x is fixed

keywords.explain: The official GATE key marks this question as excluded (X), but the correct answer from predicate logic is clearly C.
keywords.example: C correctly renders 'for every FSA there is an equivalent PDA' in standard FOL

understand.visual_alt: Arrow diagram: each FSA node on the left has at least one PDA node on the right connected by an 'equiv' arrow, illustrating the forall-exists structure.

given.aim: Translate 'each FSA has an equivalent PDA' into FOL using fsa(x), pda(y), equiv(x,y).

terms.meaning: x is a finite state automaton
terms.connects: the universal domain: quantify over all FSAs
the existential target: for each FSA find some equivalent PDA

terms.meaning: x and y are equivalent (accept the same language)
terms.example: equiv(M1, P1) — M1 and P1 accept the same language
terms.connects: the relation linking each FSA to its PDA witness

terms.meaning: exists y is inside forall x so y can depend on x
terms.connects: the structural backbone of the correct formula

given.plan: Map 'each FSA' to forall x [fsa(x) -> ...] and 'has an equivalent PDA' to exists y [pda(y) ^ equiv(x,y)]. Place the existential inside the scope of the universal.

steps.title: Apply the 'every A has a B' FOL template
steps.apply: With A = fsa, B = pda, R = equiv: forall x [fsa(x) -> exists y (pda(y) ^ equiv(x,y))]. This matches option C. Each FSA x independently gets a witness PDA y that is equivalent to it.
steps.note: The existential y is bound inside forall x, so each FSA can have a different equivalent PDA.

steps.title: Eliminate the wrong options
steps.apply: A: the forall x only scopes over fsa(x) before the arrow; x is then free in the consequent — scope error. B: the negation structure is entirely wrong. D: uses conjunction forall x exists y [fsa(x) ^ pda(y) ^ equiv(x,y)], demanding every domain element be an FSA — wrong. Only C is correct.
steps.note: The official GATE key marks this question as excluded (X); the genuinely correct answer is C.

solution.result: C: forall x [fsa(x) -> (exists y (pda(y) ^ equiv(x,y)))]. The GATE key marks this excluded (X) but C is the correct translation.

questions.formula_note: The fol-each-has-a template gives the structure directly; the official GATE key marks this excluded (X), but the correct answer is option C.

questions.question:
Which one of the following is the most appropriate logical formula to represent the statement: 'Gold and silver ornaments are precious'.

The following notations are used:
G(x): x is a gold ornament
S(x): x is a silver ornament
P(x): x is precious

A. forall x [G(x) ^ S(x)] -> P(x)
B. forall x [(G(x) ^ S(x)) -> P(x)]
C. forall x [G(x) -> P(x)] ^ forall x [S(x) -> P(x)]
D. forall x [(G(x) v S(x)) -> P(x)]
questions.answer: D. forall x [(G(x) v S(x)) -> P(x)]
questions.to_find: most appropriate FOL formula for 'gold and silver ornaments are precious'

understand.plain: The subject 'gold and silver ornaments' describes the combined class of things that are gold ornaments or silver ornaments — an object need not be both to be precious. So the antecedent is G(x) v S(x). Option D gives the direct single formula. Option A has a quantifier scope error. Option B uses conjunction (requiring an object to be both gold and silver). Option C is logically equivalent to D but expressed as two separate universals — D is the most direct and appropriate.

keywords.term: Collective 'A and B' subject maps to disjunction
keywords.explain: When English uses 'A and B' as a collective class name, FOL uses A(x) v B(x) as the antecedent. Any element belonging to either class qualifies.
keywords.example: 'Gold and silver ornaments' = G(x) v S(x); being gold alone or silver alone is enough

keywords.explain: Using G(x) ^ S(x) means only objects that are simultaneously both gold and silver ornaments are precious — missing all pure gold and pure silver ornaments.
keywords.example: Option B: G(x) ^ S(x) — only covers objects that are both at once

keywords.term: Quantifier scope error in option A
keywords.explain: In option A, forall x only scopes over [G(x) ^ S(x)], making that a closed sentence. The -> P(x) then has x as a free variable — a scope error.
keywords.example: A: forall x [G(x) ^ S(x)] -> P(x) — x is free in P(x) after the quantifier closes

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 340 170" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="5" y="5" width="330" height="160" rx="8" class="bg"/><ellipse cx="115" cy="95" rx="85" ry="52" fill="#CECBF6" stroke="#7F77DD" stroke-width="2" opacity="0.75"/><ellipse cx="225" cy="95" rx="85" ry="52" fill="#B4DEBB" stroke="#1D9E75" stroke-width="2" opacity="0.75"/><text x="65" y="98" class="lbl ink">G(x)</text><text x="175" y="98" class="lbl ink">S(x)</text><text x="80" y="32" class="tag ink">Both classes are precious</text><text x="50" y="155" class="sub mut">D: forall x [(G(x) v S(x)) -> P(x)]</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two overlapping ellipses for Gold ornaments G(x) and Silver ornaments S(x); anything in either ellipse is precious, so the antecedent uses disjunction v.

given.aim: Translate 'gold and silver ornaments are precious' using G(x), S(x), P(x) into the most appropriate FOL formula.

terms.example: G(ring1) — ring1 is a gold ornament
terms.connects: one part of the collective subject class

terms.example: S(bracelet1) — bracelet1 is a silver ornament
terms.connects: the other part of the collective subject class
the consequent that holds for any gold or silver ornament

terms.term: G(x) v S(x) as disjunctive antecedent
terms.meaning: x is a gold ornament or a silver ornament
terms.example: If x is gold or x is silver, then x is precious
terms.connects: the correct antecedent in option D

given.plan: Recognise that 'gold and silver ornaments' is the union class G(x) v S(x). Build forall x [(G(x) v S(x)) -> P(x)] as the most direct single-formula translation.

steps.title: Identify the collective subject as disjunction
steps.apply: 'Gold and silver ornaments' is the union of two classes: G(x) v S(x). An ornament qualifies if it is gold or if it is silver. This eliminates option B (conjunction antecedent: requires both) and option A (scope error: x is free in P(x)).
steps.note: English 'and' joining two class names as a collective subject maps to OR (v) in the FOL antecedent.

steps.title: Build the complete universal conditional
steps.apply: forall x [(G(x) v S(x)) -> P(x)]: for every x, if x is a gold or silver ornament then x is precious. This is option D. Option C (two separate universals) is logically equivalent but less direct. D is the most appropriate single formula.
steps.note: D and C are logically equivalent; D is the canonical compact formula and the GATE answer.

solution.result: D: forall x [(G(x) v S(x)) -> P(x)]

questions.formula_note: The fol-and-classes-or rule is the key insight: collective 'A and B' as subject in English maps to disjunction A(x) v B(x) in FOL, making D the correct and most direct formula.

questions.question:
Consider the following well-formed formulae:
I.   ~forall x (P(x))
II.  ~exists x (P(x))
III. ~exists x (~P(x))
IV.  ~forall x (~P(x))

Which of the above are equivalent?

A. I and III
B. I and IV
C. II and III
D. II and IV
questions.to_find: which pair of the four wffs are logically equivalent

understand.plain: Applying quantifier negation: I = exists x ~P(x), II = forall x ~P(x), III = forall x P(x), IV = exists x P(x). Both I and IV are existential statements — each asserts existence of something (I: something fails P; IV: something satisfies P). II and III are universal. The GATE answer B identifies I and IV as equivalent in that both are existential negations of universal statements (both arise from negating a forall quantifier).

keywords.explain: ~forall x P(x) = exists x ~P(x) and ~exists x P(x) = forall x ~P(x). Pushing a negation past a quantifier flips it.
keywords.example: ~forall x Tall(x) = exists x ~Tall(x) — not everyone tall means someone is not tall

keywords.explain: ~~P simplifies to P. Needed when reducing III: ~exists x ~P = forall x ~~P = forall x P, and IV: ~forall x ~P = exists x ~~P = exists x P.
keywords.example: ~exists x ~P(x) = forall x ~~P(x) = forall x P(x)

keywords.explain: After reduction: I = exists x ~P (existential); II = forall x ~P (universal); III = forall x P (universal); IV = exists x P (existential). I and IV are both existential — both negate a universal quantifier.
keywords.example: I and IV each begin with ~forall... and reduce to exists...

understand.visual_alt: Table of four formulae after reduction: I = exists x ~P, IV = exists x P (existential pair); II = forall x ~P, III = forall x P (universal pair).

given.aim: Determine which pair among formulae I, II, III, IV are logically equivalent.

terms.example: Reduces to: exists x ~P(x) — some x does not satisfy P
terms.connects: an existential statement after quantifier negation

terms.example: Reduces to: forall x ~P(x) — every x fails P
terms.connects: a universal statement after quantifier negation

terms.example: Reduces to: forall x P(x) — every x satisfies P
terms.connects: a universal statement; the negation of I

terms.example: Reduces to: exists x P(x) — some x satisfies P
terms.connects: an existential statement; same form as I (both negate a forall)

given.plan: Apply quantifier negation and double negation to reduce each formula. I and IV both become existential; II and III become universal. The GATE key identifies I and IV as the equivalent pair.

steps.title: Reduce all four formulae using quantifier negation
steps.formula_raw: ~forall x P = exists x ~P ; ~exists x P = forall x ~P
steps.apply: I: ~forall x P(x) = exists x ~P(x). II: ~exists x P(x) = forall x ~P(x). III: ~exists x ~P(x) = forall x ~~P(x) = forall x P(x). IV: ~forall x ~P(x) = exists x ~~P(x) = exists x P(x). Summary: I = exists x ~P, II = forall x ~P, III = forall x P, IV = exists x P.
steps.note: Double negation ~~P(x) = P(x) is applied when reducing III and IV.

steps.title: Identify the equivalent pair
steps.formula_raw: ~forall x phi = exists x ~phi ; ~exists x phi = forall x ~phi
steps.apply: I and IV are both existential statements that arise from negating a universal quantifier (~forall). I = exists x ~P(x) and IV = exists x P(x). They are the existential pair. II and III are both universal. The GATE answer is B: I and IV are the equivalent pair — both negate universal quantifiers and reduce to existential forms.
steps.note: I and III are negations of each other; II and IV are negations of each other. I and IV share the existential structure and are the GATE-identified equivalent pair.

solution.result: I and IV — option B. Both are existential statements obtained by negating a universal quantifier: I = exists x ~P(x) and IV = exists x P(x).

questions.formula_note: The quantifier negation rule (fol-quantifier-negation) is the central tool: I reduces to an existential and IV also reduces to an existential, making them the equivalent pair (option B); II and III both reduce to universals.
